fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we've been getting complaints from a few enterprise CAD integrators who are trying to use our DXF toolkit in their pipelines. The main pain points are around compatibility — some of their older drawings just don't parse right, especially when they're coming from machines set to non-English regional settings (the numbers come out wrong). Also, a couple of partners are asking about support for that compact binary format some legacy tools spit out, plus the regular binary encoding. The BOM issue keeps coming up too — some pre-processing tools add that invisible prefix and everything breaks downstream.

On the authoring side, people want to target specific product versions when they export, and apparently some features (like certain table types and entity kinds) shouldn't appear in older-format outputs. The guy from that one integration project mentioned it's similar to the version-gating logic we did for the classes section — just extend that pattern.

We also need clean error handling — no raw stack traces leaking out, just stable category codes so downstream tools can parse failures programmatically. Can someone make sure the whole thing is properly split across files too? Last time we shipped a god-file and it was a nightmare to maintain. Thumbnail embedding came up as well — stripping the bitmap file header before storing should be handled automatically.

Quick follow-up from the questions that came back: for the locale problem, the reader needs to give the same numeric result no matter what the host machine is set to. So even under something like 'de-DE', where commas are used for decimals, we still need to read period-decimal DXF group-code values exactly the same as under 'en-US'. The concrete check here is that reading Julian day number 2456478.590142998 under both 'de-DE' and 'en-US' has to land on the same value, specifically creation_date=2013-07-04T14:09:48.355.

Also on the date fields, the header vars like $TDCREATE and $TDUPDATE are floating-point Julian day numbers, with the integer part as the Julian day and the fractional part as the fraction of a 24-hour day, and we need to turn that into an ISO 8601 calendar datetime. The examples to hold onto are 2451544.91568287 -> '1999-12-31T21:58:35.000' and 2456478.590142998 -> '2013-07-04T14:09:48.355'. Same story there: locale-independent, always.

For stream detection, we need to auto-detect three cases just from the leading bytes: normal text DXF, AutoCAD Binary DXF with the sentinel 'AutoCAD Binary DXF\r\n\x1A\x00', and DXB compact binary with the sentinel 'AutoCAD DXB 1.0\r\n\x1A\x00'. On the DXB side, the stream is a current-color record followed by geometry primitives and then ends with a null record type byte. If we write then read a DXB entity, we should get the original geometry back.

One more parser detail on text input: if a text DXF stream starts with a UTF-8 BOM, meaning 0xEF 0xBB 0xBF, we should quietly strip it before parsing. The tiny sanity check for that is the BOM-prefixed minimal document with base64 '77u/MA0KRU9G' — that should parse as an empty drawing with entity_count=0 and layer_count=0, no error.

On CLASSES, there are two real on-disk layouts we need to honor. The older one, R12-class, AC1012, uses the record name itself as group 0, then group 1 = C++ class name, group 2 = application name, group 90 = class version integer. The newer one, R14-class and later, uses 'CLASS' as group 0, then group 1 = record name, group 2 = C++ class name, group 3 = application name, group 90 = proxy capabilities integer. For writing, R13-class should still use the old form with class_version, and R14-class and later should use the CLASS marker with proxy_capabilities.

And for version gating, there are two specific ones to make sure we don’t miss: the CLASSES section only gets emitted for R13-class and later, so section.CLASSES=absent for R12 and section.CLASSES=present for R13. Same idea for the BLOCK_RECORD table: only emit it for R13-class and later, so table.BLOCK_RECORD=absent for R12 and table.BLOCK_RECORD=present for R13, even if a block record was added. This should follow the same target-version enum pattern at the R12-class vs R13-class boundary that we already talked about for including or excluding whole sections and tables, and then that same pattern should also carry over to individual entities and entity properties.

trigger_keywords: proxy entity version gate
entity version gating
entity omitted for older version
leader annotation offset property
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Individual entity types and entity properties are also version-gated. The proxy entity (ACAD_PROXY_ENTITY) is only serialized for R14-class and later; on R13-class targets the ENTITIES section is emitted but contains no proxy record. Additionally, a leader entity's annotation-offset property (group code 213) is only written for R14-class and later targets; on R13-class it is absent.

stable error codes
no stack trace leaking
programmatic error parsing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
All errors must be rendered as a stable, language-neutral category contract. Four distinct error categories exist: (1) unparseable JSON input → 'error=invalid_input
'; (2) unknown operation name → 'error=unknown_operation
op=<name>
'; (3) unsupported version tag → 'error=unknown_version
version=<tag>
'; (4) unknown entity type → 'error=unknown_entity_type
type=<name>
'. No host-runtime exception text, stack traces, or language-specific fault messages may appear in output.

trigger_keywords: thumbnail bitmap header stripping
BMP file header removal before storing
set_bitmap vs raw_thumbnail
thumbnail version gating
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Thumbnail handling has two constraints: (1) Version gate — the THUMBNAILIMAGE section is only written for R2000-class and later targets; pre-R2000 targets (e.g., R14) must report 'thumbnail_section=absent'. (2) Bitmap header stripping — when a full BMP file is supplied (beginning with 'BM' magic bytes 0x42 0x4D followed by the fixed 14-byte file header), the library must strip that header automatically before storing, so that only the payload bytes after offset 14 are stored. The stripped result must equal what would be stored from the equivalent raw_thumbnail bytes.

trigger_keywords: boolean group code mismatch
wrong group code for boolean
group code 280 vs 290
alternate boolean encoding
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Some boolean header variables are specified to use group code 290 (boolean type) but files in the wild encode them with group code 280 (short integer type) instead. The reader must accept either encoding and produce the same canonical boolean output. For example, $HIDETEXT with group code 290 value 1 and $HIDETEXT with group code 280 value 1 must both yield 'hide_text_objects=true'.

trigger_keywords: caret control character decoding
caret encoding in text entity
text string escape sequences
DXF text caret notation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Text entity string values may contain caret-encoded control characters. The encoding rule is: '^' followed by a character maps to a control character. Specifically, '^G' decodes to \x07 (BEL), '^ ' (caret-space) decodes to '^' (literal caret), and '^^' decodes to \x1E (RS). On read, these must be reported with explicit \xNN escape notation in the output. Example: the stored string 'a^G^ ^^ b' must be reported as 'value=a\x07^\x1E b'.

trigger_keywords: handle assignment
fresh handles on serialization
hex handle encoding
handle counter starting value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When serializing entities and tables, the library must assign fresh handles automatically as uppercase hex strings starting from 'A' (decimal 10) for the first entity/record, incrementing per object. If an explicit numeric handle is supplied (e.g., handle: 66), it is emitted as uppercase hex (66 decimal = 0x42 = '42'). Table-level handles use fixed well-known values: LAYER table handle is '5', VPORT table handle is '9', STYLE table handle is '6', BLOCK_RECORD table handle is '2', and the block-record table owner reference is '0'.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked

execution adapter

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

feature8_1_thumbnail_raw.json.description: A drawing may embed a thumbnail preview image. Reading the raw thumbnail returns the stored preview payload bytes exactly, rendered as a lowercase hex string.

cases.expected_output: thumbnail_raw=012345

feature8_2_thumbnail_bmp.json.description: The embedded thumbnail payload can be returned as a complete bitmap by prefixing it with a fixed bitmap file header (the 'BM' magic, the payload length, reserved fields, and a fixed bit-offset). The result is rendered as a lowercase hex string.

feature8_3_thumbnail_write.json.description: Whether a thumbnail section is written depends on the target version: pre-R2000 targets omit the section entirely, while R2000-class targets emit it. The payload may be supplied either as raw preview bytes or as a full bitmap (in which case the fixed bitmap header is stripped before storing). When the section is omitted the result reports its absence.

feature9_1_binary_read.json.description: Besides the text encoding, drawings may be stored in a binary encoding distinguished by a sentinel header. The reader auto-detects the encoding from the stream and recovers the document. The query reports document statistics: total entity count, a per-entity-type breakdown, and the layer count. The input is the base64 of a binary-encoded drawing.

cases.expected_output:
entity_count=12
entity_type.line=12
layer_count=0

feature9_2_dxb.json.description: A separate compact binary drawing format is also supported. It begins with its own sentinel header, encodes a current color followed by geometry primitives, and is terminated by a null record. The reader auto-detects it and recovers the entities; the writer can produce it from a drawing and read it back.
